content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 13, 2017

Laurence S. Wiener
City Attorney
City of Beverly Hills
455 N. Rexford Drive, Room 230
Beverly Hills, CA 90210

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-17-018

Dear Mr. Wiener:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vice Mayor Nancy Krasne and Councilmember Lili Bosse regarding their duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because the substance of your questions is speculative at this time, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

Please note that we are only providing assistance under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

Must Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember Bosse recuse themselves from the following:

(1) Discussion of whether to direct the Planning Commission to make the proposed ordinance a higher priority for 2017?

(2) A conceptual discussion of the Hillside Area Ordinance, which may be similar, but not necessarily identical, to the existing design review ordinance in the Central Area?

(3) Following the Planning Commission's development of a proposed ordinance, discussion of the proposed ordinance and Hillside Area design review standards developed by the Planning Commission and potential adoption thereof.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. I-17-018
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Directing the Planning Commission to prioritize the timing of the ordinance, without
discussing the content of the proposed ordinance, would appear to be merely procedural and would
not have a foreseeable financial effect on either councilmember.

2 and 3. Since the councilmembers' respective properties will be subject to the new
ordinance, the councilmembers would have a conflict of interest. As we note below, the conclusion
may be different once an actual plan is presented. You should seek formal written advice once you
have the actual ordinance that will be considered.

FACTS

Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember Bosse (the "councilmembers") are two members of
the five-member City Council of the City of Beverly Hills. Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember
Bosse both own homes located in the Hillside Area of the City, which is an area generally located
north of Sunset Boulevard. The homes are their primary residences.

The Hillside Area of the City is a single-family zoning designation that has unique
development standards. The development standards are the same for every home in the Hillside
Area. The Hillside Area makes up 12% of the residential properties in the City and 5% of the
dwelling units in the City (due to a substantial number of multi family dwelling units in the City).

The Existing Central Area Ordinance: The Beverly Hills Municipal Code currently
requires design review of certain single family homes that are proposed to be constructed or
remodeled in the Central Area of the City, which is the area of the City other than the Hillside Area
and Trousdale Estates ("Central Area").

Pursuant to the desiem review ordinance for the Central Area of the City, if a home complies
with a pure architectural style outlined in the City's adopted "style guide," describing certain
styles of homes that are typical in Beverly Hills, the project is subject to a staff level (Track 1)
review that is limited to determining consistency with the style guide. Projects involving the
portions of the house visible from the public right-of-way that do not comply with the style guide
must undergo design review, involving consideration of the project by the City's Design Review
Commission (also referred to as Track 2).

If the alterations to the house involve only portions of the house that cannot be seen from the
public right-of-way, the house does not require any design review. The goals of requiring design
review of such homes include minimizing the appearance of scale and mass, enhancing the garden
quality of the City, and balancing the development expectations of the owner with the reasonable
expectation of privacy by the neighbors.

The Hillside Ordinance (yet to be developed): In consideration of the Central Area
ordinance which will be the model for the Hillside ordinance, you speculate:

• That the Hillside Area design review regulations might have a negative impact on the
council members' property value only if a council member redeveloped their property in a

File No. I-17-018
Page No. 3

manner that would trigger the design review ordinance (and it is your understanding that
neither intends to do so).

• The Hillside ordinance might have a positive impact on property only if the ordinance was
being applied to the property of their neighbors.

• Adoption of a Hillside Area design review ordinance would not change the development
potential of the property, and to the extent that it affects the property's zoning, that zoning
decision would affect the entire Hillside Area.

• Further, the effect of any change in the zoning would likely be limited to requiring an
additional review process and permit approval from the City for certain single family
residential projects, as in the case in the Central Area.

In a correspondence dated February 8, 2017, you provided the following additional facts:

• The proposed Hillside ordinance has not yet been drafted, therefore it is not yet known
whether it will be the same as the Central Area ordinance. You presume it will be
substantially similar.

• The purpose of design review would be to look at certain houses which may have an adverse
impact on the surrounding community. It is not intended to change the zoning restrictions.

• You do not believe the ordinance will lead to changing the development limits in the
Hillside area. You stated that the purpose of design review would be to look at certain
houses which have an adverse impact on the surrounding community. It is not intended to
change the zoning restrictions.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state
or local government agency, including city councilmembers.

Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are set forth in Section 87103.
The interest pertinent to the officials in question is set forth in Section 87103(b): "An interest in real
property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more." (Section
87103(b).)

Foreseability and Materiality

As noted above, we cannot provide formal written advice regarding an ordinance we have
not seen. Since we do not have the ordinance, this question is hypothetical in nature at this time.
The Commission advice is the application of the law (of the Act) to a specific set of facts provided

File No. I-17-018
Page No. 4

by the requestor. However, we can provide the following general guidance on the limited facts
provided.

In order for an interest in real property to be disqualifying, the specific decision in question
must have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the property.

Foreseeability:

A financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party
in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. A financial
interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial
interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as
described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an
official has a financial interest is material whenever the governmental decision:

"(1) Involves the adoption of or amendment to a general (except as
provided below) or specific plan, and the parcel is located within the proposed
boundaries of the plan;

"(2) Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning (other than a zoning
decision applicable to all properties designated in that category), annexation or
de-annexation, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district, or other
local government subdivision, or other boundaries, other than elective district
boundaries as determined by the California Citizen's Redistricting Commission or
any other agency where the governmental decision is to determine boundaries for
elective purposes;

"(3) Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that
apply to the parcel;

"(4) Authorizes the sale, purchase, or lease of the parcel;

"(5) Involves the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, permit or
other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of or improvement to the
parcel or any variance that changes the permitted use of, or restrictions placed on,
that real property. For purposes of this paragraph, any financial effect resulting
from a governmental decision regarding permits or licenses issued to the official's
business entity when operating on the official's real property shall be conclusively
analyzed under Regulation 18702.1, rather than this paragraph, without any
separate consideration for any material financial effects on the official's real
property as a result of the decision;

"(6) Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, and the parcel in which the official has an
interest will receive new or improved services that are distinguishable from

File No. I-17-018
Page No. 5

improvements and services that are provided to or received by other similarly
situated properties in the official's jurisdiction or where the official will otherwise
receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment by the decision."

The planned ordinance would not appear to be explicitly involve the officials' property as
contemplated by Regulation 18701.

In all other cases, the financial effect is foreseeable if the financial effect can be recognized
as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. You stated that you believe that
the financial effect of the adoption of a design review ordinance for the Hillside Area of the
officials' property is speculative. Of course, there are no standards at this time to analyze. However,
Regulation 18701 cautions: "A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably
foreseeable." Imposing procedural requirements on homeowners that do not currently exist would
appear to affect the use of the home. Even if the intent of the current homeowner is not to change
the residence in such a way that would be subject to the new ordinance, buyers might consider this
to be a disincentive to the purchase of the home. For example, the Central Area ordinance states:

"10-3-4401: PURPOSE AND INTENT: The council finds that the scale
and massing of buildings in the Central R-1 zone is of concern to the community
at large. Beverly Hills residential neighborhoods have traditionally been
renowned for their beauty, quality, and value and provide the city's residents with
an unparalleled quality of life. The characteristics are the product of generous
setbacks, gracious architecture, and careful attention to detail. However, since the
late 1980s emerging trends have led some owners and developers in residential
areas to disregard prevailing styles and neighborhood character in an effort to
maximize development and density. The council finds that this trend has led to
homes that greatly overpower the general local "lot to house size" ratio
("mansionization"). The mansionization of the city's residential neighborhoods
poses a serious danger that such overbuilding will degrade and depreciate the
character, image, beauty, and reputation of the city's residential neighborhoods
with adverse consequences for the quality of life of all residents. The bulk and
mass of such homes, as well as their general appearances, affect the desirability of
the immediate area and neighboring areas for residential purposes and, by so
doing, impair the benefits of occupancy of existing property in such areas, impair
the stability in value of both improved and unimproved real property in such
areas, prevent the most appropriate development of such areas, produce
undesirable conditions affecting the health, safety, comfort, and general welfare
of the inhabitants of the city, and destroy the proper relationship between the
taxable value of real property in such areas and the cost of municipal services
provided therefor. It is the purpose of this article to prevent these and other
harmful effects of such overbuilding in residential neighborhoods and, thus, to
promote and protect the health, safety, comfort, and general welfare of the
community, to promote the public convenience and prosperity, to conserve the
value of and to encourage the most appropriate development within the city's
residential neighborhoods. (Ord. 04-0-2444, eff. 5-21-2004; amd. Ord. 05-0-2487,
eff. 11-7- 2005; Ord. 06-0-2494, eff. 2-17-2006)"

File No. I-17-018
Page No. 6

This plan, imposed in the Hillside Area, would seem to affect the number of willing buyers
for a home and therefore have a foreseeable financial effect on the value of home in the area.

Materiality

Regulation 18702.2 provides:

"[T]he reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision
on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest . . . is
material whenever the governmental decision:

* * *

"(7) Would change the development potential of the parcel of real
property;

* * *

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a
financial interest;

* * *

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the
market value of the official's property."

Again, we have no proposal to analyze. However, you speculated that the adoption of design
review criteria would not change the development potential of any parcel in the Hillside Area
because no change would be made to regulations regarding building height, lot coverage, structure
size, or setbacks and that the ordinance would not change any parcel's zoning except for requiring
design review for certain types of projects equally for all parcels in the Hillside Area.

However, you also stated that the purpose of design review would be to look at certain
houses in the Hillside area which have an adverse impact on the surrounding community. This
might include looking at the councilmembers' homes. We again, have no facts as to what the
criteria would be to "look at certain houses in the Hillside area which have an adverse impact on the
surrounding community."

Moreover, we note again that the stated purpose and intent of the Central Area ordinance, the model
for the Hillside ordinance, was to prevent certain development and preserve the traditional quality

File No. I-17-018
Page No. 7

of the Hillside neighborhood. It appears the enactment of the ordinance for the purposes of limiting
certain development in the area would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's
property who live in the area subject to the ordinance.

Again, this general guidance is provided without the benefit of seeing what the planning
commission will produce and the council will consider. We encourage you to request follow up
advice once the substance of the ordinance is known for more specific formal advice.²

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

² Note that Regulation 18702.4(b)(1) states that even if a conflict of interest is present, a public official may
appear before his or her agency as any other member of the general public in the course of its prescribed governmental
function in order to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to his or her interest in real property which is
wholly owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family. Such appearance, properly made, does not
constitute making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision. (Regulation 18704(d)(2) and (4).)
Under this exception, the officials must limit their comments to their personal interests and make clear that they are not
speaking in the interest of any person or group, nor are they acting in their official capacity. (Mitchell Advice Letter,
No. 4-12-011.) Similarly, they may only discuss the decision with other council members or consultants outside of
public comments made at any public meetings. Finally, we note that a public official is not attempting to use his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision of an agency if the official communicates with the general public
or the press. (Regulation 18704(d)(4).)

Commission and potential adoption thereof.

or local government agency, including city councilmembers.

not seen. Since we do not have the ordinance, this question is hypothetical in nature at this time.

by the requestor. However, we can provide the following general guidance on the limited facts

QUESTION: Must Vice Mayor Krasne and Councilmember Bosse recuse themselves from the following: 
( 1) Discussion of whether to direct the Planning Commission to make the proposed 
ordinance a higher priority for 2017? 
(2) A conceptual discussion of the Hillside Area Ordinance, which may be similar, but not 
necessarily identical, to the existing design review ordinance in the Central Area? 
(3) Following the Planning Commission's development of a proposed ordinance, discussion 
of the proposed ordinance and Hillside Area design review standards developed by the Planning 
Commission and potential adoption thereof.

CONCLUSION: 1. Directing the Planning Commission to prioritize the timing of the ordinance, without 
discussing the content of the proposed ordinance, would appear to be merely procedural and would 
not have a foreseeable financial effect on either councilmember. 
2 and 3. Since the councilmembers' respective properties will be subject to the new 
ordinance, the councilmembers would have a conflict of interest. As we note below, the conclusion 
may be different once an actual plan is presented. You should seek formal written advice once you 
have the actual ordinance that will be considered.
